behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: SUM is a deterministic, locally-scoped function with no external dependencies. The failure boundary is the aggregation logic itself. A pure unit test can set cell values A1:A10, invoke SUM, and verify the result without I/O, multiple modules, or real infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1:A10 with numeric values, set a formula cell to =SUM(A1:A10), invoke formula evaluation, and assert the result equals the expected sum. Edge cases: empty cells, negative numbers, zero values, mixed types.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: Testing cascaded formula evaluation requires wiring up the cell dependency chain in memory, but this is still a local, deterministic operation. The failure boundary is the formula resolution and evaluation order. A unit test can create cells with formulas and verify the computed value without mocking the formula engine itself.
behaviors.test_description: Create cell A1 with value 5, cell B1 with formula =A1*2, and a third cell with formula =B1+1. Evaluate and assert the result is 11. Verify that mocking individual cells would hide failures in dependency resolution order. Test with multiple levels of nesting.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is a graph algorithm that runs in-process with no I/O or external dependencies. A unit test can create the circular reference and verify that the error is raised. Mocking the graph traversal would hide the failure, but testing the real cycle-detection logic is local and isolated.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1 and B1 with circular formulas (A1=B1+1, B1=A1+1). Attempt evaluation and assert that a CircularReferenceError is raised, not an infinite loop. Test with longer cycles (A→B→C→A) and self-references (A=A+1).

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP is a deterministic lookup function with two code paths (match/no match). Both paths are local and deterministic. A unit test can create a lookup table and verify both cases without mocking the lookup logic itself or needing external infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table (e.g., product ID → price) in cells. Call =VLOOKUP(key, table_range, column_index, FALSE) with a key that exists and verify the correct value is returned. Call with a non-existent key and verify a #N/A error is raised. Test type coercion (string '123' vs. number 123), case sensitivity, and wrong column index.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: Nested IF evaluation is deterministic control flow in a local scope. The failure boundary is the condition evaluation and branch selection logic. A unit test can verify all branches without I/O, mocks, or external infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: For the formula =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"), test with A1=5 (expect "low"), A1=15 (expect "medium"), and A1=25 (expect "high"). Test deeply nested conditions (5+ levels), type coercion in conditions (string "20" vs. number 20), and mixed condition operators.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE is a deterministic string function with special handling for empty cells. A unit test can create cells with mixed values (empty, string, number) and verify the concatenation logic without external dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1="Hello", A2=(empty), A3="World". Call =CONCATENATE(A1, A2, A3) and verify the result is "HelloWorld" (not "Hello World"). Test with numeric values (auto-conversion to string), multiple separators, all-empty input, and all-filled input. Verify empty cells are skipped, not converted to "null" or "".

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require multiple sheet objects to be wired together in the workbook. While deterministic, the failure boundary crosses module/object boundaries (Sheet1 → workbook → Sheet2 → cell resolution). A unit test of a single sheet would not catch failures in sheet lookup or namespace resolution. The test must instantiate multiple sheets and verify the reference is correctly resolved across them.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with two sheets (Sheet1 and Sheet2). Set Sheet2!A1=10. In Sheet1, create a formula =Sheet2!A1+1 and evaluate it. Verify the result is 11. Test error cases: referencing a non-existent sheet (expect error), invalid reference syntax, and sheet name case sensitivity. Test that Sheet1 can reference multiple cells in Sheet2.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: Topological recalculation requires the real dependency graph, not mocks. If you mock the graph traversal or cell dependencies, the test would pass without verifying correct recalculation order. This crosses multiple module boundaries (cell A → dependency graph → all dependent cells). A unit test of a single formula cannot catch failures in the recalculation engine or evaluation order.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1=1, B1=A1*2 (=2), C1=B1+A1 (=3). Change A1 to 5 and trigger recalculation. Verify B1 updates to 10, then C1 updates to 15 (in that order). Test with wider dependency graphs (A→B→C→D→E) and diamond dependencies (A←B, A←C, D←B, D←C). Verify no cell is evaluated twice and none retain stale values.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: Undo requires coordinating formula storage, undo stack state, and dependent cell recalculation. These span multiple modules. Mocking the undo stack or cell storage would hide failures in state restoration. The test must actually edit a formula, trigger recalculation of dependents, undo, and verify both the formula text and all dependent values are restored—this requires wiring multiple subsystems together.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1=1, B1=A1*2 (=2), C1=B1+A1 (=3). Edit B1's formula to =A1*3 and verify recalculation (B1=3, C1=4). Undo the edit and verify B1's formula reverts to =A1*2, B1 recalculates to 2, and C1 recalculates to 3. Test undo stack depth (multiple edits, undo all the way back), redo behavior, and undo with no prior edits (should be no-op).

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires real disk I/O and binary format serialization/deserialization. Mocking file operations would hide real failures: byte-order bugs, floating-point rounding during serialization, format version incompatibilities, and file encoding issues. Only real read/write cycles can catch precision loss or data corruption. The test boundary is the actual file system, not in-process code.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with cells containing formulas, floats (e.g., 1/3 ≈ 0.333...), strings, and cross-sheet references. Save to disk, load back, and verify all cell values and formulas are byte-for-byte identical. Test floating-point precision (1/3 should not become 0.33 or 0.333333333333334 after round-trip). Test with large workbooks, special characters in formulas, and binary data edge cases. Verify file integrity (corrupted file raises error on load, not silent data loss).
